title: Cell Biology: The Basics (Quiz-RPG Roguelike Campaign)
description: A roguelike quiz-RPG campaign that maps foundational cell biology into themed encounters: organelles and cellular respiration.

acts.title: Act I: The Cell and Its Organelles
acts.summary: Explore how the cell’s boundaries, command center, and energy hubs work—setting the stage for respiration.

encounters.title: The Cell Membrane Gateworks
encounters.enemyFlavor: A shimmering phospholipid wall with selectively permeable control—plus protein channels and pumps that only let the right travelers through.
encounters.subTopic: The Cell Membrane (phospholipid bilayer, selectively permeable, embedded proteins as channels and pumps)

questions.prompt: What is the cell membrane made of, according to the source text?
sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::The Cell Membrane (phospholipid bilayer, selectively permeable, embedded proteins as channels and pumps)
questions.sourceQuote: The cell membrane is a phospholipid bilayer
questions.explanation: The source explicitly states that the cell membrane is a phospholipid bilayer.

questions.prompt: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, meaning it controls which substances enter and leave the cell.
sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::The Cell Membrane (phospholipid bilayer, selectively permeable, embedded proteins as channels and pumps)
questions.sourceQuote: It is selectively permeable, controlling which substances enter and leave the cell.
questions.explanation: This is true because the source says the membrane controls what enters and leaves the cell.

questions.prompt: What do the embedded proteins in the cell membrane do?
sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::The Cell Membrane (phospholipid bilayer, selectively permeable, embedded proteins as channels and pumps)
questions.sourceQuote: Embedded proteins act as channels and pumps
questions.explanation: The source says these proteins function as channels and pumps that move ions and molecules across the membrane.

options.text: They act as channels and pumps to move ions and molecules across the membrane
They store genetic information inside the membrane
They make the membrane rigid like a wall
They prevent the cell membrane from surrounding the cell

encounters.title: The Nucleus: Genetic Command
encounters.enemyFlavor: A double-membrane sanctuary of DNA, watched by nuclear pores that regulate what crosses between nucleus and cytoplasm.
encounters.subTopic: The Nucleus (stores DNA; directs cellular activities; nuclear envelope; nuclear pores regulating movement)

rewards.name: Flash of Insight
rewards.description: Reveal one wrong answer once per encounter.

questions.prompt: What does the nucleus store as the cell's genetic material?
sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::The Nucleus (stores DNA; directs cellular activities; nuclear envelope; nuclear pores regulating movement)
questions.sourceQuote: stores the cell's genetic material as DNA
questions.explanation: The source states that the nucleus stores the cell's genetic material as DNA.

questions.prompt: The nuclear pores regulate the movement of molecules between the nucleus and the cytoplasm.
sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::The Nucleus (stores DNA; directs cellular activities; nuclear envelope; nuclear pores regulating movement)
questions.sourceQuote: nuclear pores that regulate the movement of molecules
questions.explanation: Yes—according to the source, nuclear pores regulate movement between the nucleus and the cytoplasm.

sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::The Nucleus (stores DNA; directs cellular activities; nuclear envelope; nuclear pores regulating movement)
questions.sourceQuote: It is enclosed by the nuclear envelope
questions.explanation: The source directly says the nucleus is enclosed by the nuclear envelope.

encounters.title: Mitochondria, the Powerhouse
encounters.enemyFlavor: Inner-membrane cristae hum with oxidative phosphorylation—turning aerobic inputs into ATP output.
encounters.subTopic: Mitochondria (ATP production via aerobic respiration; inner membrane folded into cristae)

rewards.name: Scholar's Focus
rewards.description: +25% damage while your streak is 3 or higher.

questions.prompt: What process do mitochondria use to produce ATP?
sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::Mitochondria (ATP production via aerobic respiration; inner membrane folded into cristae)
questions.explanation: The source states that mitochondria produce ATP through aerobic respiration.

questions.prompt: Mitochondria are often called the powerhouse of the cell.
sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::Mitochondria (ATP production via aerobic respiration; inner membrane folded into cristae)
questions.sourceQuote: Often called the powerhouse of the cell
questions.explanation: The source explicitly says mitochondria are often called the powerhouse of the cell.

questions.prompt: What is the function of the inner membrane folds called cristae?
sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::Mitochondria (ATP production via aerobic respiration; inner membrane folded into cristae)
questions.sourceQuote: folded into cristae that increase the surface area
questions.explanation: Cristae increase the surface area for the reactions of oxidative phosphorylation.

options.text: They increase surface area for oxidative phosphorylation reactions.
They store genetic material for the cell.
They break down proteins into amino acids.
They transport oxygen out of the cell.

acts.title: Act II: Cellular Respiration
acts.syllabusTopic: Glycolysis, Krebs cycle, and the electron transport chain
acts.summary: Step through the energy pathway: from glucose breakdown, to the Krebs cycle’s transfers, to the electron transport chain’s proton pumping and ATP synthase power.

encounters.title: Glycolysis: Cytoplasm Splitter
encounters.enemyFlavor: A cytoplasmic skirmish that cleaves one glucose into two pyruvate—no oxygen required, just a modest ATP spark.
encounters.subTopic: Glycolysis (cytoplasm; breaks glucose into two pyruvate; produces a small net gain of two ATP; does not require oxygen)

sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::Glycolysis (cytoplasm; breaks glucose into two pyruvate; produces a small net gain of two ATP; does not require oxygen)
questions.explanation: The source explicitly states that glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm.

questions.prompt: What is the net ATP gain from glycolysis?
sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::Glycolysis (cytoplasm; breaks glucose into two pyruvate; produces a small net gain of two ATP; does not require oxygen)
questions.sourceQuote: It produces a small net gain of two ATP
questions.explanation: The passage says glycolysis produces a small net gain of two ATP.

options.text: A small net gain of two ATP
A large net gain of ten ATP
A net loss of two ATP

sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::Glycolysis (cytoplasm; breaks glucose into two pyruvate; produces a small net gain of two ATP; does not require oxygen)
questions.explanation: The source directly states that glycolysis does not require oxygen.

encounters.title: The Krebs Cycle: Matrix Alchemy
encounters.enemyFlavor: In the mitochondrial matrix, pyruvate-derived molecules are oxidized—carbon dioxide is released while energy is handed to electron carriers.
encounters.subTopic: The Krebs Cycle (mitochondrial matrix; oxidizes pyruvate-derived molecules; releases CO2; transfers energy to NADH and FADH2)

rewards.name: Flash of Insight
rewards.description: Reveal one wrong answer once per encounter.

questions.prompt: According to the source, what are mitochondria the site of?
sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::The Krebs Cycle (mitochondrial matrix; oxidizes pyruvate-derived molecules; releases CO2; transfers energy to NADH and FADH2)
questions.sourceQuote: Mitochondria are the site of ATP production through aerobic respiration.
questions.explanation: The source explicitly says mitochondria are the site of ATP production through aerobic respiration.

questions.prompt: The inner membrane of mitochondria is folded into cristae that increase surface area.
sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::The Krebs Cycle (mitochondrial matrix; oxidizes pyruvate-derived molecules; releases CO2; transfers energy to NADH and FADH2)
questions.sourceQuote: they have an inner membrane folded into cristae that increase the surface area
questions.explanation: True, because the source states that the inner membrane is folded into cristae and that these increase surface area.

questions.prompt: What is the stated role of the cristae in mitochondria?
sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::The Krebs Cycle (mitochondrial matrix; oxidizes pyruvate-derived molecules; releases CO2; transfers energy to NADH and FADH2)
questions.sourceQuote: cristae that increase the surface area for the reactions of oxidative phosphorylation
questions.explanation: The cristae increase surface area specifically for the reactions of oxidative phosphorylation, as stated in the source.

options.text: They increase surface area for the reactions of oxidative phosphorylation
They store ATP for later release into the nucleus
They convert light energy into glucose
They prevent aerobic respiration from occurring

encounters.title: Electron Transport Chain: Proton Pump Siege
encounters.enemyFlavor: Electrons stream along the inner mitochondrial membrane; protons are pumped to build a gradient that ATP synthase exploits—oxygen stands as the final electron acceptor.
encounters.subTopic: The Electron Transport Chain (inner mitochondrial membrane; electron-driven proton pumping; gradient powers ATP synthase; oxygen is final electron acceptor)

rewards.name: Scholar's Focus
rewards.description: +25% damage while your streak is 3 or higher.

questions.prompt: According to the source, what are mitochondria the site of?
sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::The Electron Transport Chain (inner mitochondrial membrane; electron-driven proton pumping; gradient powers ATP synthase; oxygen is final electron acceptor)
questions.sourceQuote: Mitochondria are the site of ATP production
questions.explanation: The source directly states that mitochondria are the site of ATP production through aerobic respiration.

questions.prompt: The inner membrane of mitochondria is folded into cristae.
sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::The Electron Transport Chain (inner mitochondrial membrane; electron-driven proton pumping; gradient powers ATP synthase; oxygen is final electron acceptor)
questions.sourceQuote: they have an inner membrane folded into cristae
questions.explanation: The source explicitly says that mitochondria have an inner membrane folded into cristae.

questions.prompt: Why does the source say cristae are important?
sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::The Electron Transport Chain (inner mitochondrial membrane; electron-driven proton pumping; gradient powers ATP synthase; oxygen is final electron acceptor)
questions.sourceQuote: cristae that increase the surface area for the reactions of oxidative phosphorylation
questions.explanation: Cristae matter because they increase surface area, which helps support the reactions of oxidative phosphorylation.

options.text: They increase the surface area for the reactions of oxidative phosphorylation
They store oxygen for later use in the nucleus
They break down glucose in the cytoplasm
They prevent ATP from being produced too quickly

questions.prompt: What cellular structure is described as being folded into cristae?
sourceChunkIds: doc_cell_biology::The Electron Transport Chain (inner mitochondrial membrane; electron-driven proton pumping; gradient powers ATP synthase; oxygen is final electron acceptor)
questions.sourceQuote: they have an inner membrane folded into cristae
questions.explanation: The source identifies the inner membrane as the part that is folded into cristae.
